  ┌─ WHAT YOU STILL NEED ─────────────────────────────────────
  │  1. │     DSR below threshold — more live track record is the
  │     only fix. Every month of live returns consistent with
  │     backtest adds significant weight.
  │
  │  2. │     Walk-forward instability — high Sharpe variance across
  │     regimes suggests the edge may be period-specific.
  │     Investigate which folds underperformed and why.
  │
  │  3. │     Sharpe below 2.5 — acceptable but an allocator will
  │     ask what is suppressing it. Ensure slippage
  │     assumptions are realistic.
  │
  │  4. │     Calmar below 3.0 — return per unit of MaxDD is below
  │     institutional benchmark. Either CAGR needs to improve
  │     or MaxDD needs to tighten.
  │
  │  5. │     Sortino below 4.0 — more downside volatility than
  │     expected for this Sharpe. Check whether losses are
  │     clustering or spreading uniformly.
  │
  │  6. │     Regime robustness — Sharpe decay or CAGR ratio
  │     suggests performance deteriorated in the second half
  │     of the dataset. Characterise what changed and whether
  │     it is structural.
  │
  │  7. │     Narrow parameter plateau — optimal params sit on a
  │     ridge rather than a broad stable region. Real-world
  │     execution deviation from exact parameters may degrade
  │     performance.
  │
  │  8. │     MC % losing above 2% — more path-ordering sensitivity
  │     than ideal. The positive expectation may be partially
  │     sequence-dependent.
  │
  │  9. │     Slippage resilience — Sharpe degrades materially at 2×
  │     base slippage. Live execution costs may be higher than
  │     assumed. Stress-test against wider spreads.

  ┌─ WHAT YOU STILL NEED ─────────────────────────────────────

  │     only fix. Every month of live returns consistent with
  │     backtest adds significant weight.
  │
  │  2. │     Walk-forward instability — high Sharpe variance across
  │     regimes suggests the edge may be period-specific.
  │     Investigate which folds underperformed and why.
  │
  │  3. │     Sharpe below 2.5 — acceptable but an allocator will
  │     ask what is suppressing it. Ensure slippage
  │     assumptions are realistic.
  │
  │  4. │     Narrow parameter plateau — optimal params sit on a
  │     ridge rather than a broad stable region. Real-world
  │     execution deviation from exact parameters may degrade
  │     performance.
  │
  │  5. │     Slippage resilience — Sharpe degrades materially at 2×
  │     base slippage. Live execution costs may be higher than
  │     assumed. Stress-test against wider spreads.

  │  expectation is fragile to return ordering. Investigate
  │  concentration.
  │     Goal: <1%  (<0.5% ideal)   Actual: 100.00%
  │
  │  ❌ Slippage Resilience  —  0/7
  │  Sharpe 0.480 at 2× slippage — fragile to cost increases.
  │  Backtest slippage assumptions may be optimistic.
  │     Goal: Sharpe≥1.8 at 2× slippage  (≥2.5 ideal)   Actual: 0.480
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  ┌─ WHAT YOU HAVE — DUE DILIGENCE CHECKLIST ─────────────────
  │  ✅ DSR and MTL — statistical validity proven (35.60% DSR)
  │  ✅ MC reshuffle (2,000 iterations) and block bootstrap stress tests
  │  ✅ Neighbor plateau test — joint parameter robustness confirmed
  │  ✅ Slippage, cost curve, and capacity curve — operational concerns addressed
  │  ✅ Shock injection — tail risk quantified under artificial stress scenarios
  │  ✅ Drawdown episode analysis — depth, duration, and recovery tracked
  │  ✅ Regime robustness — first-half vs second-half performance split
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  ┌─ WHAT YOU STILL NEED ─────────────────────────────────────
  │  1. │     DSR below threshold — more live track record is the
  │     only fix. Every month of live returns consistent with
  │     backtest adds significant weight.
  │
  │  2. │     Walk-forward instability — high Sharpe variance across
  │     regimes suggests the edge may be period-specific.
  │     Investigate which folds underperformed and why.
  │
  │  3. │     Sharpe below 2.5 — acceptable but an allocator will
  │     ask what is suppressing it. Ensure slippage
  │     assumptions are realistic.
  │
  │  4. │     Calmar below 3.0 — return per unit of MaxDD is below
  │     institutional benchmark. Either CAGR needs to improve
  │     or MaxDD needs to tighten.
  │
  │  5. │     Sortino below 4.0 — more downside volatility than
  │     expected for this Sharpe. Check whether losses are
  │     clustering or spreading uniformly.
  │
  │  6. │     Regime robustness — Sharpe decay or CAGR ratio
  │     suggests performance deteriorated in the second half
  │     of the dataset. Characterise what changed and whether
  │     it is structural.
  │
  │  7. │     Narrow parameter plateau — optimal params sit on a
  │     ridge rather than a broad stable region. Real-world
  │     execution deviation from exact parameters may degrade
  │     performance.
  │
  │  8. │     MC % losing above 2% — more path-ordering sensitivity
  │     than ideal. The positive expectation may be partially
  │     sequence-dependent.
  │
  │  9. │     Slippage resilience — Sharpe degrades materially at 2×
  │     base slippage. Live execution costs may be higher than
  │     assumed. Stress-test against wider spreads.
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  ┌─ BOTTOM LINE ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  │  The strategy scores 18/100. There are 8 outright
  │  failures and 1 marginal results that require attention
  │  before this is deployable. Failing metrics: Deflated
  │  Sharpe Ratio (DSR), Walk-Forward Stability (CV), Sharpe
  │  Ratio, Calmar Ratio, Sortino Ratio, Regime Robustness,
  │  MC Reshuffle % Losing, Slippage Resilience. Focus on the
  │  gating metrics first — Sharpe and DSR must both clear
  │  before the other scores matter.
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  ┌─ WHAT YOU HAVE — DUE DILIGENCE CHECKLIST ─────────────────
  │  ✅ DSR and MTL — statistical validity proven (48.48% DSR)
  │  ✅ MC reshuffle (2,000 iterations) and block bootstrap stress tests
  │  ✅ Neighbor plateau test — joint parameter robustness confirmed
  │  ✅ Slippage, cost curve, and capacity curve — operational concerns addressed
  │  ✅ Shock injection — tail risk quantified under artificial stress scenarios
  │  ✅ Drawdown episode analysis — depth, duration, and recovery tracked
  │  ✅ Regime robustness — first-half vs second-half performance split
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  ┌─ WHAT YOU STILL NEED ─────────────────────────────────────
  │  1. │     DSR below threshold — more live track record is the
  │     only fix. Every month of live returns consistent with
  │     backtest adds significant weight.
  │
  │  2. │     Walk-forward instability — high Sharpe variance across
  │     regimes suggests the edge may be period-specific.
  │     Investigate which folds underperformed and why.
  │
  │  3. │     Sharpe below 2.5 — acceptable but an allocator will
  │     ask what is suppressing it. Ensure slippage
  │     assumptions are realistic.
  │
  │  4. │     Calmar below 3.0 — return per unit of MaxDD is below
  │     institutional benchmark. Either CAGR needs to improve
  │     or MaxDD needs to tighten.
  │
  │  5. │     Sortino below 4.0 — more downside volatility than
  │     expected for this Sharpe. Check whether losses are
  │     clustering or spreading uniformly.
  │
  │  6. │     Regime robustness — Sharpe decay or CAGR ratio
  │     suggests performance deteriorated in the second half
  │     of the dataset. Characterise what changed and whether
  │     it is structural.
  │
  │  7. │     Narrow parameter plateau — optimal params sit on a
  │     ridge rather than a broad stable region. Real-world
  │     execution deviation from exact parameters may degrade
  │     performance.
  │
  │  8. │     Slippage resilience — Sharpe degrades materially at 2×
  │     base slippage. Live execution costs may be higher than
  │     assumed. Stress-test against wider spreads.
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  ┌─ BOTTOM LINE ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  │  The strategy scores 27/100. There are 6 outright
  │  failures and 2 marginal results that require attention
  │  before this is deployable. Failing metrics: Deflated
  │  Sharpe Ratio (DSR), Walk-Forward Stability (CV), Sharpe
  │  Ratio, Calmar Ratio, Regime Robustness, Slippage
  │  Resilience. Focus on the gating metrics first — Sharpe
  │  and DSR must both clear before the other scores matter.
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  │
  │  ❌ Sortino Ratio  —  0/8
  │  Sortino 1.574 — downside volatility is too high relative
  │  to returns. Distribution needs improvement.
  │     Goal: ≥4.0  (≥8.0 ideal)   Actual: 1.574
  │
  │  ❌ Regime Robustness  —  0/8
  │  n/a — significant performance decay in OOS half. This is
  │  the most important gap to investigate before deployment.
  │     Goal: decay<30%, CAGR ratio≥0.60   Actual: n/a
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  ┌─ SUPPORTING METRICS  (3/23)  Grade: D ──────────────────
  │
  │  ⚠  Neighbor Plateau (Parameter Robustness)  —  3/8
  │  68.5% of neighbors within 0.5 Sharpe — moderate
  │  robustness. Small deviations from exact params may
  │  degrade live performance.
  │     Goal: ≥70%  (≥80% ideal)   Actual: 68.5%
  │
  │  ❌ MC Reshuffle % Losing  —  0/8
  │  100.00% of reshuffles lost money — the positive
  │  expectation is fragile to return ordering. Investigate
  │  concentration.
  │     Goal: <1%  (<0.5% ideal)   Actual: 100.00%
  │
  │  ❌ Slippage Resilience  —  0/7
  │  Sharpe 0.557 at 2× slippage — fragile to cost increases.
  │  Backtest slippage assumptions may be optimistic.
  │     Goal: Sharpe≥1.8 at 2× slippage  (≥2.5 ideal)   Actual: 0.557
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  ┌─ WHAT YOU HAVE — DUE DILIGENCE CHECKLIST ─────────────────
  │  ✅ DSR and MTL — statistical validity proven (38.94% DSR)
  │  ✅ MC reshuffle (2,000 iterations) and block bootstrap stress tests
  │  ✅ Neighbor plateau test — joint parameter robustness confirmed
  │  ✅ Slippage, cost curve, and capacity curve — operational concerns addressed
  │  ✅ Shock injection — tail risk quantified under artificial stress scenarios
  │  ✅ Drawdown episode analysis — depth, duration, and recovery tracked
  │  ✅ Regime robustness — first-half vs second-half performance split
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  ┌─ WHAT YOU STILL NEED ─────────────────────────────────────
  │  1. │     DSR below threshold — more live track record is the
  │     only fix. Every month of live returns consistent with
  │     backtest adds significant weight.
  │
  │  2. │     Walk-forward instability — high Sharpe variance across
  │     regimes suggests the edge may be period-specific.
  │     Investigate which folds underperformed and why.
  │
  │  3. │     Sharpe below 2.5 — acceptable but an allocator will
  │     ask what is suppressing it. Ensure slippage
  │     assumptions are realistic.
  │
  │  4. │     Calmar below 3.0 — return per unit of MaxDD is below
  │     institutional benchmark. Either CAGR needs to improve
  │     or MaxDD needs to tighten.
  │
  │  5. │     Sortino below 4.0 — more downside volatility than
  │     expected for this Sharpe. Check whether losses are
  │     clustering or spreading uniformly.
  │
  │  6. │     Regime robustness — Sharpe decay or CAGR ratio
  │     suggests performance deteriorated in the second half
  │     of the dataset. Characterise what changed and whether
  │     it is structural.
  │
  │  7. │     Narrow parameter plateau — optimal params sit on a
  │     ridge rather than a broad stable region. Real-world
  │     execution deviation from exact parameters may degrade
  │     performance.
  │
  │  8. │     MC % losing above 2% — more path-ordering sensitivity
  │     than ideal. The positive expectation may be partially
  │     sequence-dependent.
  │
  │  9. │     Slippage resilience — Sharpe degrades materially at 2×
  │     base slippage. Live execution costs may be higher than
  │     assumed. Stress-test against wider spreads.
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  ┌─ BOTTOM LINE ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  │  The strategy scores 18/100. There are 8 outright
  │  failures and 1 marginal results that require attention
  │  before this is deployable. Failing metrics: Deflated
  │  Sharpe Ratio (DSR), Walk-Forward Stability (CV), Sharpe
  │  Ratio, Calmar Ratio, Sortino Ratio, Regime Robustness,
  │  MC Reshuffle % Losing, Slippage Resilience. Focus on the
  │  gating metrics first — Sharpe and DSR must both clear
  │  before the other scores matter.
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  │  ✅ DSR and MTL — statistical validity proven (45.59% DSR)

  │  ✅ Slippage, cost curve, and capacity curve — operational concerns addressed
  │  ✅ Shock injection — tail risk quantified under artificial stress scenarios
  │  ✅ Drawdown episode analysis — depth, duration, and recovery tracked

  ┌─ WHAT YOU STILL NEED ─────────────────────────────────────
  │  1. │     DSR below threshold — more live track record is the
  │     only fix. Every month of live returns consistent with
  │     backtest adds significant weight.
  │
  │  2. │     Walk-forward instability — high Sharpe variance across
  │     regimes suggests the edge may be period-specific.
  │     Investigate which folds underperformed and why.
  │
  │  3. │     Sharpe below 2.5 — acceptable but an allocator will
  │     ask what is suppressing it. Ensure slippage
  │     assumptions are realistic.
  │
  │  4. │     Calmar below 3.0 — return per unit of MaxDD is below
  │     institutional benchmark. Either CAGR needs to improve
  │     or MaxDD needs to tighten.
  │
  │  5. │     Sortino below 4.0 — more downside volatility than
  │     expected for this Sharpe. Check whether losses are
  │     clustering or spreading uniformly.
  │
  │  6. │     Regime robustness — Sharpe decay or CAGR ratio
  │     suggests performance deteriorated in the second half
  │     of the dataset. Characterise what changed and whether
  │     it is structural.
  │
  │  7. │     Narrow parameter plateau — optimal params sit on a
  │     ridge rather than a broad stable region. Real-world
  │     execution deviation from exact parameters may degrade
  │     performance.
  │
  │  8. │     Slippage resilience — Sharpe degrades materially at 2×
  │     base slippage. Live execution costs may be higher than
  │     assumed. Stress-test against wider spreads.
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  ┌─ BOTTOM LINE ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  │  The strategy scores 26/100. There are 7 outright

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  STEP 6 — PBO  (Probability of Backtest Overfitting)
  CSCV  ·  Bailey, Borwein, Lopez de Prado & Zhu (2015)
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  PBO — PROBABILITY OF BACKTEST OVERFITTING  [All filter modes]
  CSCV method · Bailey et al. (2015) · J. Computational Finance
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  Strategies (N)        : 6
  Trading days (T)      : 398  (sub-period len=24d, S=16)
  Combinations C(S,S/2) : 12,870

  PBO                   : 0.002  (0.2%)
  Grade                 : ⚠  LOW RESOLUTION (N=6 < 10 — see warnings)
  Performance Degradation (PD) : +0.0045  Sharpe drop train→test
  Probability of Loss (POL)    : 0.003  (0.3%)  (test Sharpe of winner < 0)
  Discrete logit values (6) : -2.3979  -1.0986  -0.3365  +0.3365  +1.0986  +2.3979

  ── Training-window win counts ──
  A - Calendar Filter                       12844×  ( 99.8%)  █████████████████████████████████████████████████
  A - Tail + Dispersion                        24×  (  0.2%)  
  A - No Filter                                 1×  (  0.0%)  
  A - Tail + Disp + Vol                         1×  (  0.0%)  
  A - Tail Guardrail                            0×  (  0.0%)  
  A - Volatility                                0×  (  0.0%)  

  ── Logit(ω) distribution (λ_c = log(rank/(N−rank))) ──
  Mean  : +2.3894    Median: +2.3979
  Stdev : 0.1403     Range : [-1.0986, +2.3979]
  λ < 0 :   21 / 12870  = PBO
  λ = 0 :    0 / 12870  (winner ranked at exact median)
  λ > 0 : 12849 / 12870

  ── Warnings ──
  ⚠  LOW RESOLUTION: N=6 strategies means logit(ω) can only take 6
       discrete values (-2.3979, -1.0986, -0.3365, +0.3365, +1.0986,
       +2.3979). PBO=0.002 reflects which of these 6 buckets the
       training winner falls into — not a continuous probability. Grade
       bands are not reliable at N < 10.

  ⚠  STRUCTURAL BIAS: when the N strategies are filter variants of
       the same base strategy, unfiltered variants have more active
       trading days per sub-period, which mechanically inflates their
       sub-period Sharpe. This causes unfiltered variants to win more
       training splits even when filtered variants are stronger over
       the full period. Interpret win counts with this in mind.

  ⚠  Strategy 'A - Calendar Filter' won 100% of training splits —
       near-deterministic winner suggests a dominant strategy, which
       may indicate insufficient strategy diversity for a meaningful
       PBO.

  ── Interpretation ──
  With N=6 strategies the logit(ω) distribution has only 6
  discrete values, so PBO grade bands (Low / Moderate / High) are
  not statistically meaningful here.
  
  What this result DOES tell you:
    · In 0% of train/test splits the filter with the best
      in-sample Sharpe did NOT rank in the top half out-of-sample.
      → Filter selection by in-sample Sharpe alone is unstable.
    · The unfiltered variant dominates training splits because it
      has more active trading days per sub-period, mechanically
      inflating its sub-period Sharpe (active-day structural bias).
    · This is expected behaviour, not evidence of overfitting.
  
  What this result does NOT tell you:
    · It does NOT mean the underlying strategy is overfit.
    · POL = 0% means the training winner ALWAYS had positive
      test Sharpe — the strategy was profitable in every OOS window.
  
  Recommended action:
    · To get a meaningful PBO, run CSCV across a large grid of
      independently-tuned parameter configurations (N ≥ 20 ideally),
      not across filter variants of the same base strategy.
    · Use POL and PD as the primary overfitting diagnostics here.
